request.question: 51824 Mikeanderson is an asteroid named for the recipient of what United States award?

title: 31641 Cevasco
1767 Lampland
17198 Gorjup
1827 Atkinson
Michael P. Anderson
2227 Otto Struve
26858 Misterrogers
25924 Douglasadams
51824 Mikeanderson
2007 McCuskey
sentences: 31641 Cevasco, provisional designation 1999 GW, is a stony asteroid from the inner regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 3.3 kilometers in diameter.
It was discovered on 6 April 1999, by the Lincoln Near-Earth Asteroid Research project at Lincoln Laboratory's Experimental Test Site in Socorro, New Mexico, United States.
The asteroid was named for Hannah Cevasco, a 2015 Broadcom MASTERS awardee.
1767 Lampland, provisional designation 1962 RJ, is an Eoan asteroid from the outer regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 15 kilometers in diameter.
It was discovered on 7 September 1962, by astronomers of the Indiana Asteroid Program at Goethe Link Observatory in Indiana, United States.
The asteroid was named after American astronomer Carl Lampland.
17198 Gorjup, provisional designation 2000 AA, is a stony Flora asteroid and asteroid pair from the inner regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 2.7 kilometers in diameter.
It was discovered on 3 January 2000, by the Lincoln Near-Earth Asteroid Research team at the Lincoln Laboratory Experimental Test Site in Socorro, New Mexico, United States.
The asteroid was named for Slovenian Niko Gorjup, a 2003 awardee of the ISEF contest.
1827 Atkinson, provisional designation 1962 RK, is a background asteroid from the central regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 9 kilometers in diameter.
It was discovered on 7 September 1962, by IU's Indiana Asteroid Program at Goethe Link Observatory near Brooklyn, Indiana, United States.
The asteroid was named after British astronomer Robert d'Escourt Atkinson.
Michael Phillip Anderson (December 25, 1959 – February 1, 2003) was a United States Air Force officer and NASA astronaut.
Anderson and his six fellow crew members were killed in the Space Shuttle "Columbia" disaster when the craft disintegrated during its re-entry into the Earth's atmosphere.
Anderson served as the payload commander and lieutenant colonel in charge of science experiments on the Columbia.
Anderson is a recipient of the Congressional Space Medal of Honor.
2227 Otto Struve, provisional designation 1955 RX, is an asteroid from the inner regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 4.7 kilometers in diameter.
The asteroid was discovered on 13 September 1955, by the Indiana Asteroid Program at Goethe Link Observatory near Brooklyn, Indiana, United States.
It was named after Russian astronomer Otto Struve.
26858 Misterrogers is a Mars crossing asteroid named after children's television host Fred Rogers.

Rogers, who had a lifelong fascination with the sky and astronomy—he obtained a pilot's license while still in high school —also produced with the Carnegie Science Center in Pittsburgh a planetarium show called "The Sky above Mister Rogers' Neighborhood", which is still shown at many planetariums across the United States.
25924 Douglasadams, provisional designation 2001 DA, is a Nysian asteroid from the inner regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 2.4 kilometers in diameter.
It was discovered on 19 February 2001, by astronomers of the Lincoln Near-Earth Asteroid Research at the Lincoln Laboratory's Experimental Test Site in New Mexico, United States.
The asteroid was named for novelist Douglas Adams.
51824 Mikeanderson (2001 OE ) is an asteroid named for astronaut Mike Anderson, who was killed in the STS-107 ("Columbia") space shuttle reentry disaster on February 1, 2003.
51824 Mikeanderson was discovered on July 19, 2001, at Palomar Observatory by the JPL Near Earth Asteroid Tracking Program.
2007 McCuskey, provisional designation 1963 SQ, is a carbonaceous asteroid from the inner regions of the asteroid belt, approximately 22 kilometers in diameter.
It was discovered on 22 September 1963, by astronomers of the Indiana Asteroid Program at Goethe Link Observatory near Brooklyn, Indiana, United States.
The asteroid was later named after American astronomer Sidney McCuskey.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: 51824 Mikeanderson is an asteroid named for the recipient of what United States award?
hop1_docs: 51824 Mikeanderson | 51824 Mikeanderson (2001 OE ) is an asteroid named for astronaut Mike Anderson, who was killed in the STS-107 ("Columbia") space shuttle reentry disaster on February 1, 2003.  51824 Mikeanderson was discovered on July 19, 2001, at Palomar Observatory by the JPL Near Earth Asteroid Tracking Program.

Milken Educator Award | The Milken Educator Awards is an educator recognition program in the United States that provides unrestricted grants of $25,000 cash to teachers deemed successful, in surprise ceremonies.  Established in 1985 and first presented in 1987, the initiative of the Milken Family Foundation has traveled to schools across the United States presenting awards to over 2,600 teachers, averaging around 30-40 teachers per year.  "Teacher Magazine" nicknamed the program the "Oscars of Teaching."  The award currently gives $25,000 in unrestricted funds to teachers who are early in their career, or mid-career, to reward them "for what they have achieved—and for the promise of what they will accomplish in the future."  Recipients are ambushed at school assemblies or other public events to be publicly celebrated with the surprise announcement of the awards.  For example, in January 2016, a Hawaii high school science teacher was "shocked" to receive the award, given at a school-wide assembly.

Elmer S. Best Memorial Award | Elmer S. Best Memorial Award is an annual award named in memory of the founder of the Pierre Fauchard Academy.  It was established by the Board of Trustees in 1962.  Recipients of the award must be members of the dental profession and from outside the United States.

Randall Kenan | Randall Kenan (born March 12, 1963) is an American author of fiction and non-fiction.  Raised in a rural community in North Carolina, Kenan has focused his fiction on what it means to be black and gay in the southern United States.  Among his books is the collection of short stories "Let the Dead Bury Their Dead", which was named a New York Times Notable Book in 1992.  Kenan is the recipient of a Guggenheim Fellowship, a Whiting Award and the John Dos Passos Prize.

Bispham Memorial Medal Award | The Bispham Memorial Medal Award was an award for operas written in English which was presented annually by the American Opera Society of Chicago from 1921 until 1932.  The award was named for baritone David Bispham, who was a great proponent of performing opera in English in the United States.  It was traditionally awarded to American composers, frequently for an opera on an American subject.  It was funded, in part, by composer Eleanor Everest Freer, who was also one of its recipients (for "The Legend of the Piper").  Other recipients include (alphabetically by author):

Clara Ingram Judson Award | The Clara Ingram Judson Award (officially: Clara Ingram Judson Memorial Award) is given annually to the most creative children's writing in the Midwest United States since 1960 by the Society of Midland Authors.  It was named after Clara Ingram Judson, the first recipient of the award.

List of Presidential Medal of Freedom recipients | This is an alphabetized, partial list of recipients of the Presidential Medal of Freedom, grouped by the aspect of life in which they are/were renowned.  The Presidential Medal of Freedom is awarded by the President of the United States "for especially meritorious contribution to (1) the security or national interests of the United States, or (2) world peace, or (3) cultural or other significant public or private endeavors"; it is awarded to individuals selected by the President or recommended to him by the Distinguished Civilian Service Awards Board.  The only exception to the rule for the sitting president choosing those to be honored, was that the first recipients were selected by President John F. Kennedy before his assassination, and formally awarded by his successor in office, Lyndon B. Johnson.  President Barack Obama awarded 123 Medals, the most ever, followed by President Ronald Reagan with 102 Medal recipients.
hop1_titles: List of Presidential Medal of Freedom recipients
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- 51824 Mikeanderson — [1] "an asteroid named for astronaut Mike Anderson"
- astronaut Mike Anderson — [1] "astronaut Mike Anderson"
- STS-107 / "Columbia" — [1] "STS-107 (\"Columbia\")"
- death date token — [1] "February 1, 2003"
- discovery date token — [1] "July 19, 2001"
- Palomar Observatory — [1] "Palomar Observatory"
- JPL Near Earth Asteroid Tracking Program — [1] "JPL Near Earth Asteroid Tracking Program"
- Milken Educator Awards — [2] "Milken Educator Awards is an educator recognition program"
- Elmer S. Best Memorial Award — [3] "Elmer S. Best Memorial Award is an annual award"
- Guggenheim Fellowship; Whiting Award; John Dos Passos Prize — [4] "recipient of a Guggenheim Fellowship, a Whiting Award and the John Dos Passos Prize."
- Bispham Memorial Medal Award — [5] "The Bispham Memorial Medal Award was an award"
- Clara Ingram Judson Award — [6] "Clara Ingram Judson Award (officially: Clara Ingram Judson Memorial Award)"
- Presidential Medal of Freedom — [7] "The Presidential Medal of Freedom is awarded by the President of the United States"

Ambiguities:
- "Mike Anderson" could refer to multiple people (passages only show the astronaut context in [1]); no disambiguating page is provided.
- Multiple U.S. awards are present in the passages (Milken, Presidential Medal of Freedom, Guggenheim, etc.), but none are linked to Mike Anderson in these passages.

Bridge / missing fact:
- Missing the specific United States award name(s) that Mike Anderson (the astronaut) was a recipient of. The passages do not state which U.S. award(s), if any, he received.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch the astronaut's biography page: "Michael P. Anderson" (Wikipedia page title) or "Mike Anderson (astronaut)"
- Fetch targeted awards info: search "Michael P. Anderson awards" or "Mike Anderson astronaut awards and honors"
- If needed, check relevant award lists: "List of recipients of the Congressional Space Medal of Honor" and "List of recipients of the Presidential Medal of Freedom"
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
hop2_docs: Michael Anderson Jr. | Michael Joseph Anderson Jr. (born 6 August 1943) is an English actor.

1994 MTV Europe Music Awards | The first MTV Europe Music Awards were held on November 24, 1994, at Brandenburg Gate, Berlin, Germany, five years after the removal of the Berlin Wall.  Hosted by Tom Jones, the show featured performances by Aerosmith, Björk, Roxette, Take That and George Michael.  Presenters included East 17, Jean Paul Gaultier, Naomi Campbell, Pamela Anderson and model Helena Christensen, who kissed INXS's Michael Hutchence live on stage.
Michael Anderson Pereira da Silva | Michael Anderson Pereira da Silva (born February 16, 1983 in São Caetano do Sul) better known as simply Michael, is a professional Brazilian football player.

Michael P. Anderson | Michael Phillip Anderson (December 25, 1959 – February 1, 2003) was a United States Air Force officer and NASA astronaut.  Anderson and his six fellow crew members were killed in the Space Shuttle "Columbia" disaster when the craft disintegrated during its re-entry into the Earth's atmosphere.  Anderson served as the payload commander and lieutenant colonel in charge of science experiments on the Columbia.  Anderson is a recipient of the Congressional Space Medal of Honor.

Michael Redman (singer) | Michael M. Redman (born April 15, 1945) is an American singer who was a member of television's "The Lawrence Welk Show" from 1980 to 1982 as part of the trio Gail, Ron and Michael, with Ron Anderson and Anderson's wife, Gail Farrell.

The Changing Room | The Changing Room is a 1971 play by David Storey, set in a men's changing room before, during and after a rugby league football game.  It premiered at the Royal Court Theatre on 9 November 1971, directed by Lindsay Anderson.  The 1973 Broadway production, directed by Michael Rudman, won several awards including the New York Drama Critics' Circle award for Best Play and the Tony Award for Best Featured Actor for John Lithgow.

Michael Anderson (basketball) | Michael Levin Anderson (born March 23, 1966) is an American former basketball player for Drexel University, the NBA, Spain's professional Liga ACB, the Continental Basketball Association, the Greek A1 League and the Turkish Basketball League.
response.summary_2: 51824 Mikeanderson is named for astronaut Mike Anderson, a recipient of the Congressional Space Medal of Honor.
